data.title: My Favorite Strategy To Trade Long-Term Treasury Bonds
data.description:
Introduction

In a diversified portfolio, government bonds are often considered a great way to diversify and hedge against crashes. However, years like 2022 show that bonds and stocks can fall together as well. The following strategy solves this problem by using signals to go long and short on bonds via Direxion Daily 20 Year Plus Treasury Bull 3X Shares (NYSEARCA:TMF) and ProShares Ultrashort 20+ Year Treasury (NYSEARCA:TBT), enabling investors to hedge their portfolios more effectively. Additionally, the strategy outperforms the market and produces positive alpha in comparison to both long-term bonds and SPY SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY).

The Strategy

One of the most important indicators for bonds, especially long-term bonds, are interest rates, which generally are a function of inflation. Since inflation can be seen in commodities, it is worth watching indices such as iPath Bloomberg Commodity Index Total Return ETN (DJP).

The first part of the strategy is to compare the 30-day performance of SPY to that of DJP. If DJP outperforms SPY, I open a long position in TBT to short bonds as this means that commodity prices and inflation are rising faster than asset prices.

On the other side, if SPY outperforms DJP, this means that assets are rising faster than inflation. However, as this still can mean higher inflation, DJP also has to have a negative 30-day performance to open our long position in TMF to buy long-term treasury bonds.

The last factor I consider is the trend. For this strategy, I define the trend as rather TLT (iShares 20+ Year Bond ETF) is above or below its 200-day moving average. Positions in TMF are only allowed if TLT is above the line, while positions in TBT are only allowed if TLT is below the line.

Overall, I update this signal once a week.

Performance

As can be seen in Figure 1, the strategy produces positive alpha in comparison to both SPY (stocks) and TLT (bonds). Additionally, it has a negative beta to SPY indicating that it works as a hedging strategy.

Figure 2 gives information about the nominal performance of the strategy. It outperforms both TLT and SPY in average performance and Sharpe ratio. While it has a higher volatility than TLT, its drawdown is lower than that of the base index.

Since I advertised the strategy in the beginning as a way to hedge one portfolio and as being able to give directional signals, one has to put this to the test by analyzing the strategy performance during a crash where bonds performed well (2020) and one where they performed badly (2022).

The 2020 Crash

If you look at Figure 3, you can see the strategy's performance during the first quarter of 2020 when the big crash occurred. As you can see, the strategy showed a performance of 66.5%, far better than the TLT which only yielded 22.5%, while SPY was down 23.8%. Additionally, the strategy only showed half of the TLT drawdown, while its Sharpe ratio is almost twice as high. In Figure 4, you can also see the asset allocation of the strategy during the crash with orange being cash and purple being TMF.

The 2022 Crash

While the 2020 crash was deflationary and therefore caused bonds to rise, the 2022 crash was inflationary. As the FED raised interest rates, bonds fell, leading to the normal bond hedge not working.

Figure 5 shows the performance of the strategy during the first half of 2022 when the crash occurred. The strategy rose by 41.8%, while both TLT and SPY fell by almost 20% during the same period.

Figure 6 also shows the asset allocation during this crash. Here the colors have the same meaning with red being TBT.

Risks

As with every other quantitative strategy, this one also runs the risk of the old saying: "Past performance is no indicator of future performance". However, the reasoning behind the strategy makes sense in the first place, which is why the critique is only partly true. Additionally, I created the idea for the strategy first and only backtested it afterward, which means that the risk of data mining should not be an issue. The only thing added after the original idea was the trend approach as a criterion for fewer trades.

Concerning the leveraged ETFs used in the strategy, one should consider that leveraged ETFs like TMF and TBT carry much higher risks, and are not suitable options for risk-averse investors. It is important for you to read the fund's prospectus of both TMF and TBT as well as SEC's related article to gain a full understanding of these risks

Conclusion

The presented strategy gives investors an easy approach to trading long-term bonds that beats both the bond and the stock market. It is especially useful to hedge against crashes more efficiently as it also protects investors from inflationary crashes where bonds usually fall.

data.title: i-80 Gold: Progress In Capitalization Strategy, Development Of Gold Projects In Nevada Drive Stock Recovery
data.description:
A 'Hold' Rating for Shares of i-80 Gold Corp.

This analysis recommends a “Hold” rating on the publicly traded shares of i-80 Gold Corp. (TSX:IAU:CA).

About its own profile, i-80 Gold Corp. says:

“i-80 Gold Corp. is a Nevada-focused mining company with a goal of achieving mid-tier gold producer status through the development of multiple deposits within the Company's advanced-stage property portfolio with processing at i-80's centralized milling facilities.”

This Is How It Has Been Since The Previous Coverage

We are slightly lowering the rating to Hold from Buy of the previous article as we would like to advise a more cautious stance despite the potential of this small gold producer to develop into a mid-sized gold producer in an extremely favorable environment for the gold price. In fact, in the previous article, we predicted a rise in the price of gold due to strong demand for safe haven assets. Since then, gold has risen by 70 to 72 percent. When it comes to i-80 Gold Corp instead, this stock is still lagging behind schedule, and this delay is reflected in a loss of traction of -49.12% compared to the concurrent growth of 50.10% for the S&P 500. (Since the last analysis on September 8, 2024, and at the time of this article, these are the changes in the market value of the securities.) The Fed's tight interest rate policy is a major blow to sentiment around this stock as i-80 Gold Corp. needs financing capital to grow and develop into a mid-sized gold producer. The Fed's delay in cutting interest rates due to stickier inflation than initially thought was a negative surprise for the markets, especially for the i-80 Gold Corp.’s shares, for the reasons just highlighted. In fact, as things seemed to be moving toward a gradual easing of borrowing costs, starting with the Fed's first jumbo rate cut of 50 bps on September 18, 2024, the shares of i-80 Gold Corp. were well on their way to a recovery. Then in mid-November, the hype surrounding the “Trump trade,” with his uncertain multi-proposition plan to boost US growth, dealt another blow to sentiment. Trump’s proposals, which caused renewed inflation concerns, led to several trading sessions of pullbacks in the price of gold, sliding to a monthly low. This led to a correction in the share price of i-80 Gold Corp., as i-80 Gold Corp.’s shares are correlated to the yellow metal. Then the interest rate cut on December 18, 2024, lifted morale with recovery effects that could be observed in the stock price. As a kind of confirmation that things were as we described, that is, with the Fed dampening the market sentiment due to its “higher-for-longer” stance on rate policy, could be given by the plunge of i-80 Gold Corp. shares following the announcement of the 60.6-million-unit public offering. Likely, the announcement was perceived by market participants as another sign of the temporary impracticality of the main avenues for growth capital.

But now things appear to be restoring to better times: Last month we saw important developments in the relationship between i-80 Gold Corp. and its primary lender, and that bodes well for progress in the development of its gold mining projects. There is value in this company's portfolio, but for it to be brought to the surface, financing conditions will need to become less and less restrictive. This will happen, but it will still require patience, so in our opinion, investors would be wise to be cautious and prefer to assign a buy view to other opportunities for the time being.

Nevertheless, There Is A Correlation Between Gold and i-80 Gold Corp

The idea of having i-80 Gold Corp. shares in the portfolio is to benefit from their positive correlation with changes in gold prices, and the correlation, in our view, is fueled by bullish catalysts that will be highlighted later in this analysis. Before we go any further, we want to explain what we mean by "positive correlation." It is that when the price of gold is rising, i-80 Gold's share price is likely to be dominated by bullish sentiment in the stock market. This type of relationship, which links i-80 Gold Corp.'s stock to spot gold price fluctuations, is represented by the yellow area chart that is mostly above zero in the "I-80 GOLD CORP vs. XAUUSD FXOPEN chart" below.

The Upside Catalysts Are As Follows: The Gold Price Outlook

The price of gold per ounce is still expected to move up, and Goldman Sachs recently raised its price target for gold to 2025 – the opinion of a US bank major among leading analysts. Yahoo Finance wrote:

“Gold's (GC=F) glittering run in 2025 may have more room to rise higher, Goldman Sachs believes. On Tuesday, the investment bank lifted its year-end price target for gold to $3,100 an ounce, from $2,890 previously. Goldman said "structurally higher" central bank demand will add 9% to the price of gold by the end of the year, also helped by a slight boost from ETF holdings.”

There is also something important, about the likely impact on gold prices from concerns over President Trump's fear of spiraling tariffs, highlighted by Goldman and that Yahoo Finance added as follows:

“However, if policy uncertainty — including tariff fears — stays high, higher speculative positioning for longer could push gold prices as high as $3,300 an ounce by year-end," Goldman strategist Lina Thomas wrote in a note to clients.”

This means that with the expected continued speculation driving the gold price even higher and the fact that i-80 Gold Corp.'s share price is positively correlated with the gold price, it would make no sense to put i-80 Gold Corp.'s stock on the radar for a short-selling strategy that - contrary to Goldman Sachs' vision - needs a bearish trend to be as profitable as possible.

Therefore, investors may want to hold shares of i-80 Gold Corp. – and be prepared to add to the position after a sharp decline (currently this is not the case as the stock is overbought) – while the price of gold is on an uptrend. We recommend holding the shares of i-80 Gold Corp. because there are clear and likely uncontroversial reasons to do so. But while the rise in the gold price and the upside catalysts highlighted later are very good reasons, they do not close the circle, yet, and probably represent, among other things, the easiest part of the task of laying the foundation for our recommendation. Therefore, to arrive at a balanced recommendation, we have not only outlined the arguments for the Hold recommendation but also provided some evidence that the counterarguments do not represent the right stance at this time. In fact, we also want to draw the attention of market participants to comparing a contrarian investment strategy with its success rate according to the latest available statistics. Traders considering betting against the sentiment influenced by the rising gold price with i-80 Gold Corp. will be surprised to read that short-selling strategies result in the need to build up the necessary reserves to mitigate very poor performance.

Supported by the statistics, it is generally believed that short-selling strategies are not recommended at this time. In fact, 2025 got off to a rocky start for short sellers, according to data from S3 Partners provided to Yahoo Finance. Traders “have lost $73 billion between US and Canadian markets to start 2025,” Yahoo Finance wrote, noting that traders tried to bet too heavily on contrarian trends while equity markets in general were showing a healthy uptrend by any metric, including gold and gold stocks.

More on Upside Catalysts: Income Is On Track To Get Better

The higher gold price creates a favourable environment for i-80 Gold Corp.'s earnings, a key driver of sentiment toward North American equities, to rise sharply. The company doesn't want to miss this growth opportunity to gradually improve its income lines if, given the momentum of the average realized gold price on track to move further above $2,290 per ounce in 9M 2024 (up 19% year-over-year), the operations on their side can generate more sales volume and mineralized material sales than 7,186 ounces and 29,041 tons, respectively, in 9M of 2024. Thus, both revenues of $27.1 million (down 7% year-on-year) and net income of negative $0.26 per share (worsening from the year-on-year loss of $0.13 per share) for the first nine months of 2024 are well positioned to improve, and sentiment on the stock can only gain. The company has therefore adopted a new development plan that will involve the construction of five gold mines and will transform the company into a mid-sized gold producer with annual production of 400,000 to 500,000 ounces of gold in the coming years.

More on Upside Catalysts: The Drilling Potential

The company is well positioned to achieve its growth objectives with results from over 24,400 metres of drilling indicating the potential for further development of high-grade mineralisation from at least three of the company's five most advanced gold projects. Exploration could provide additional upside to a catalyst-rich 2025, starting as early as the current quarter when the company plans to announce PEAs (“Preliminary Economic Assessments”) for all five of its most advanced gold projects: Granite Creek (combined underground and open pit), Ruby Hill Complex (combined Archimedes underground and Mineral Point open pit) and the McCoy-Cove underground project.

Update On The McCoy Cove Project

Earlier this month, i-80 Gold Corp. updated the 2025 PEA for the Cove Project, an underground exploration project located on the Battle Mountain-Eureka Trend in Northern Nevada, confirming the potential to become the first of three underground mines that will form i-80 Gold's future production base and feed high-grade material to a fully permitted central processing plant. The drilling has the potential to make Cove an even larger and longer-lived underground producer than the updated PEA indicates: 100,000 ounces of gold per year over 8 years at cash costs of $1,194 per ounce and All-In-Sustaining (“AISC”) costs of $1,303 per ounce, based on updated 311,000 ounces at 8.2 grams per tonne ("g/t") in indicated gold resources and 1.16 Moz at 8.9 g/t in inferred gold mineral resources. The company will provide a further update through a feasibility study before the end of this year. The Cove underground gold mine will produce the yellow metal cheaper than the $1,388 per ounce. We are using this price level as a benchmark to assess whether production at Cove will be cost-effective or not. In a previous article, we wrote:

“This is the average AISC per ounce of the world's gold miners, based on each operator's most recent available figures from 2024. This AISC estimate is highlighted in a report by senior geologist and industrial professor Xuan-Ce Wang at the Guangzhou Institute of Geochemistry of the Chinese Academy of Sciences."”

Based on a gold price of $2,175/oz, the after-tax net present value (“NPV” at a 5% discount rate) is $271 million or $0.62/share, giving an internal rate of return (“IRR”) of 30%. However, if the gold price increases to $2,900/oz, which is more in line with the current value of $2,931.57/oz (as of this writing), the after-tax NPV (at a 5% discount rate) increases to $582 million or $1.33/share (versus the current share price of $1.14/share of i-80 Gold Corp.) and the IRR increases to 52%. In addition to the benefit of comparing the NPV/share to the share price at the current gold price/ounce, which is expected to trade even higher, the IRR estimates also promise to improve the sentiment around i-80 Gold Corp.'s share price. Even an IRR of 30% or more would make Cove a robust gold mining project compared to other metal projects we have found for our analysis so far.

The estimated capital to build the mine is $157 million.

Update on the Archimedes Underground Project

Earlier this month, i-80 Gold Corp. updated the 2025 PEA for the Archimedes Underground Project at the southeast end of the Battle Mountain-Eureka Trend in northeast Nevada, confirming the potential to become another major pillar of future production for i-80 Gold Corp. Assuming the gold price remains robust as analysts expect, there is still room for improvement in the economics of this mine, although Archimedes is probably not the best project in the company's portfolio in terms of distance to the mill and grade of mineralization. The updated 2025 PEA assumes the Archimedes Underground Project will produce 100,000 ounces of gold annually for 10 years, but the operation is significantly less cost-effective than the Cove Project. Estimated LOM cash costs are $1,769 per ounce and AISC is $1,893 per ounce. However, drilling and technical studies can still reduce costs and extend the life of operations. The company will release a feasibility study in 2028, allowing ample time to work on “Mine Life” and smooth costs. Also, the significantly higher ounces of inferred resources that can be added by drilling compared to indicated resources provide optimism that better estimates may be forthcoming. The Archimedes Underground Project has mineral resources of 436,000 ounces at 7.6 g/t in indicated gold mineral resources and 988,000 ounces at 7.3 g/t in inferred gold mineral resources. The fact that the company has left the mineral grade of the inferred resources unchanged is a good sign in our view. When there is little or no economic viability data to support their estimates, geologists cannot draw any conclusions about resources other than inferring them. Their approach is conservative. However, as the pool of inferred mineral resources remains very large and their inferred metallic gold grade is high and consistent with higher-tier indicated resources, there is reason to hope that the company can achieve even better cost estimates and a longer mine life by developing these inferred resources.

Based on $2,175/oz, this gold price assumption results in an after-tax net present value (“NPV” at a 5% discount rate) of $127 million, or an after-tax NPV (at 5% rate) of $0.29/share, yielding an IRR of 23%. But as the spot gold price rises to $2,900/oz, the NPV becomes $1.32/share versus i-80 Gold Corp.’s share price of $1.14/share (at the time of this writing), yielding an IRR of 75%. As the gold price continues to rise, the potential of ever-improving sentiment around the share price is gaining momentum as NPV and IRR become more enticing.

To build this mine, the company needs $49 million.

"In the coming weeks, we look forward to releasing updated PEAs for Granite Creek (both open pit and underground) and the Ruby Hill Complex (Archimedes underground and Mineral Point open pit)," stated Richard Young, Chief Executive Officer of i-80 Gold.", earlier this month.

This means that the company is going to release PEAs for Granite Creek and Ruby Hill Complex (Mineral Point open pit) following McCoy-Cove and Archimedes.

More on Upside Catalysts: Metal-Rich State of Nevada and Proximity to Major Infrastructure Mitigates the Investment Risk

This portfolio of highly mineralized gold deposits is strategically located in a gold-rich state such as Nevada where the Sprott ESG Mining Risk Heat Map 2025, as for all mining regions in North America, assigns a “minimal risk” to conduct exploration and mining activities. The investment risk for the lenders, stock market participants interested in the company and all other stockholders of i-80 Gold is further mitigated by the fact that the company claims to be “the third largest resource holder in the state of Nevada.” The company also says that Nevada is one of the “largest gold producers in the world.” In addition, the company emphasizes that its complex is located near major transportation and communication routes that provide easy access to end markets in Nevada. As such, i-80 Gold Corp. is physically well-positioned to capture significant value from its “undeveloped high-grade gold mineralization.”, the company also says.

More on Upside Catalysts: Recapitalization Is Well Underway To Sustain Development Projects

The company is pursuing a recapitalization strategy to support its development projects. The recapitalization is well underway and includes the opportunity to extend capital utilization through a six-month extension of the convertible loan agreement with Orion Mine Finance from December 31, 2025, to June 30, 2026, as i-80 Gold Corp. had $239.04 million of debt as of September 30, 2024. The balance sheet showed cash and short-term investments of $21.8 million, which, together with funds raised from operations and the ability granted by Orion to defer upcoming gold and silver delivery obligations, will support the business, including covering 12-month interest expense of approximately $34.9 million as of the third quarter of 2024. The company is relying on the issuance of new shares to shareholders, board members and the open market to raise approximately $20 million (C$29 million).

The Stock Price Is Trading High, Hold It For Now and Always Look For Dips to Add Shares

Shares of i-80 Gold Corp., which trades on the TSX under the symbol IAU:CA, were at CAD$1.14/share, giving the stock a market capitalization of CAD$500.25 million at the time of writing.

The shares are trading at high price levels as they are close to pulling their head completely out of the shackles of the MA Ribbon. However, shares are also closer to the lower boundary than the upper boundary of the 52-week range of CAD$0.47/share to CAD$2.20/share.

But the 14 Relative Strength indicator of 75.52x, which shows overbought levels, says there is not much room to close the gap from here to the upper boundary of the 52-week range. This means shares will need to suffer a small setback before the recovery continues “after issuing a going concern warning” Seeking Alpha reported in mid-November 2024.

However, these concerns have been allayed as the company has reported progress in extending the term of funds with its lenders to keep the development projects going and the company is also finally issuing the PEAs.

The headwind that could pave the way for a decline in i-80 Gold Corp. stock price is investors tempering their bullishness on gold as hopes for a rate cut from the Federal Reserve fade. For now, disinflation appears to be proceeding at a slower pace, and some Fed officials even believe it's wiser to keep rates a little higher and a little longer than expected to be more certain that inflation won't cause dangerous setbacks. Also, Trump's plans (deregulation, higher corporate taxes, tougher crackdowns on illegal immigration, and increased tariffs) threaten to reignite inflation. If the price per ounce of non-income-yielding gold is hurt by fading hopes for rate cuts, shares of this stock could be poised to make lower prices.

Conclusion

This analysis recommends a “Hold” rating on the publicly traded shares of i-80 Gold Corp., a Nevada-focused mining company with the goal of achieving mid-tier gold producer status by developing multiple deposits.

Investment risk is mitigated by Nevada’s gold-rich nature and proximity to key infrastructure connected to the gold end-markets. The price of the gold metal is also rising, driven by its safe-haven properties amid a challenging global geopolitical climate and the Fed’s 100 basis point rate cut in 2024 and another cut expected in the second half of 2025.

This stock experienced a sharp crash in mid-November 2024 as markets began to sense that the company might have trouble continuing its operations in Nevada. Things have changed since then as the company has been able to agree to an extension of the loan capital it has available and is also issuing shares to the market and to its largest shareholders and directors to raise capital to sustain day-to-day operations. The negative sentiment was likely compounded by short-selling and this analysis also explains why traders should refrain from doing so for the time being. The company has also made progress as it finally issues PEAs for its key gold mine development projects. The stock price on the market recovered from the ongoing concern warning in mid-November but is now too close to overbought levels.

Investors should hold their shares for now and wait for a pullback before strengthening their position, which makes sense as recent progress with lenders and PEAs is probably fueling a positive correlation with the promising outlook for gold prices.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Marvell Q4 Preview: XPU Architecture With Integrated Co-Packaged Optics
data.description:
I assigned a ‘Strong Buy’ to Marvell Technology (NASDAQ:MRVL) in December 2024, highlighting the production ramp in custom silicon. I think Marvell will disclose more information about its latest XPU architecture with integrated co-packaged optics technology in Q4. I am taking advantage of the recent stock market pull-back to buy more Marvell. I reiterate a ‘Strong Buy’ rating with a one-year target price of $140 per share.

XPU Architecture with Integrated Co-packaged Optics

On January 6th, 2025, Marvell announced its progress of its custom XPU architecture with co-packaged optics technology. The integrated optics allow faster connections between XPUs and distance 100 times longer than electrical cabling, as disclosed in the announcement.

As discussed in my previous articles, both Marvell and Broadcom (AVGO) are the main custom ASIC silicon providers for cloud Hyperscalers. For instance, Amazon (AMZN) has been working with Marvell for several years to develop AWS Trainium, its in-house AI chips for AI training and inference workloads. AWS Trainium2 has been adopted by Amazon and other partners. In Amazon’s recent earnings call, the management disclosed that Amazon collaborated with Anthropic to build Project Rainier, a cluster of Trainium 2 ultra servers for AI workloads. Amazon plans to launch its next-generation Trainium 3 chip later this year.

As such, I think the breakthrough is important for Marvell as their ASIC technology could continue to support Hyperscalers and AI model companies in developing their own GPU/XPU chips, providing cost-effective alternatives to Nvidia (NVDA) and AMD (AMD).

During the Q3 FY25 earnings call, Marvell noted a stronger-than-expected production ramp for its custom silicon partnership with Hyperscalers. The management expressed strong confidence in the custom silicon growth in the future. I believe Marvell’s growth will accelerate in the near future, driven by increased demands for custom silicon

Outlook & Valuation

Marvell is set to report its Q4 result on March 5th after the market close, guiding for around 26% year-over-year growth in revenue, as detailed in the table below.

Data center business represented 72.6% of total revenue and grew by 98% year-over-year in Q3 FY25. It is evident that the custom silicon and data center have become the primary growth driver for Marvell in the near future. Assuming its data center business grows by 90% in Q4, aligned with the recent growth trajectory, Marvell’s data center business would contribute around 35% growth to the topline. As such, I think Marvell’s Q4 guidance is quite conservative.

For the near-term growth, I am considering the following factors:

  • Data Center: I calculate the segment will represent more than 70% of total revenue for FY25. As custom silicon production ramp-up has just begun, I forecast Marvell’s data center business will continue growing by 50% in FY26 and FY27, contributing 35% growth to the topline.
  • Enterprise Networking: I forecast 10% revenue growth, in line with the networking and routers market growth.
  • I continue to forecast Carrier infrastructure and Consumer businesses will grow by 5% annually, aligned with historical average.
  • Putting together, I calculate Marvell will deliver 40% revenue growth in FY26 and FY27. As AI market matures, I anticipate Marvell’s revenue will decelerate to 23% from FY28, followed by 20% from FY30 and 15% from FY32.
  • Marvell has invested heavily in its R&D for custom silicon technology, and there were many up-front expenses when collaborating with cloud Hyperscalers. Notably, they spent 34.4% of total revenue in R&D in FY24. I anticipate its margin expansion will be primarily driven by R&D normalization and improvement in COGS. I model 310bps annual margin expansion, driven by 200bps from R&D normalization and 80bps from COGS improvement and 30bps from reduction in SG&A expenses.
  • The cost of equity is calculated to be 14.4%, assuming: risk-free rate 3.6%; beta 1.8; equity risk premium 6%.

I calculate the free cash flow from equity (FCFE) as follows:

Discounting all the future FCFE to the end of FY25, the one-year target price is calculated to be $140 per share, as per my estimates.

Key Risks

I anticipate there will be some questions about tariff impact during the incoming earnings call. As China represents 43% of total revenue, any tariff or geopolitical risks could pose significant business impacts on Marvell’s business growth. As reported by the media, Marvell withdrew its China R&D team in late 2022. However, Marvell still has significant business exposure in China.

Verdict

I think Marvell is likely to provide a strong growth guidance for FY26, driven by the strong production ramp up of its custom silicon technology. I reiterate a ‘Strong Buy’ rating with a one-year target price of $140 per share.

data.title: Cadence Design Systems: Strong Compounder But Lacks The Growth I Desire
data.description:
Cadence Design Systems (NASDAQ:CDNS) is an interesting company that I consider fairly valued by my reverse discounted cash flow analysis. In the DCF, the terminal multiple is the most important factor that, in the case of Cadence, should persist at a high level due to the nature of its business and competition. Cadence shows strong abilities and differentiation, while its investment in AI tools should only increase the ARPU. Despite the good attributes of Cadence, I will assign a hold rating because it lacks the growth I desire and is fairly valued.

Background

Cadence, from a business perspective, is a safe company to buy. It is very hard for Cadence to be lower in price, in 5 years from now. It can happen if there is a hard crisis close to the end of the 5 years. The company specializes in design software, emulation and prototyping for semiconductors. Despite the semiconductor industry being very cyclical, typically, the industry members' R&D expenses (what generates revenue for Cadence) stay high in downturns, with the goal of investing for the future.

Cadence main competitor is Synopsys (SNPS), and both share a large percentage of the design semiconductor market. Cadence competes in analog and digital design but has a bigger moat around analog design. However, analog fabless companies such as Analog Devices (ADI) are projected to grow in low teens while Arm (ARM), a digital design IP powerhouse is projected to grow in low twenties. Thus, digital specialization could provide more growth than analog.

Strategy

Here I want to write about part of Cadence strategy in one point.

Build and defend the moat of analog design standard. Cadence is considered in the leading position of analog design. Virtuoso is much more mature for analog design than Synopsys counterpart, has a bigger community of developers that creates network effects. Some developers are claiming that the analog community is very attached to the product. Additionally, If we leave out the most advanced node digital chips, that some estimates say that each cost 725 million dollars to develop, the common analog chips are much harder to design than the common digital ones. Moreover, Analog chips are much harder to design than those who accomplish the same task digitally. This happens because analog chips are much more susceptible to noise (small, undesired variations in voltage), generated by, for example, auto-induced heat. By being more difficult to design there should be more optimizations that Cadence can do to increase its solutions ARPU, and the customer will be more inclined to pay more for the best solution, which is Cadence’s. In a recent press release published on Seeking Alpha, MediaTek is using the most advanced features of Cadence’s analog design to improve design productivity by 30%. The applied tools are the newest versions of Virtuoso studio and Specter. These tools enable analog design and simulation, respectively. Since Cadence spends a lot of revenue in R&D, there should be timely improvements in the Virtuoso analog design solution and Spectre simulation features, further increasing Cadence's moat.

Financials

Cadence has superior gross margins in comparison with Synopsys. However, the Cadence gross margins have been decreasing significantly, especially if we compare the last December quarter with the same period a year ago. In contrast, Synopsys gross margins have been stable in the past five quarters. Cadence margins are superior to Synopsys because its IP segment, which carries lower gross margin, is less developed than Synopsys’.

If we compare the FCF/Employed Capital for the past five quarters, we can see that Cadence is much more consistent. If we compare the mean of the last five quarters, Cadence has been outperforming in this very important quality metric.

If we compare the days to convert working capital into revenue, influenced by receivables, inventory and unearned revenue, Synopsys has a significant lower value showing, overall, better bargaining power and inventory management.

Both companies have negative net debt. Synopsys has 5.3% of its market cap in cash and short-term investments, while Cadence has 3.5% of market cap in cash and short-term investments.

Finally, Cadence is significantly more valued than Synopsys. This could be due to higher gross margins, and better FCF/EC. However, CDNS’s gross margins have been decreasing. If the trend continues, we could see its multiple contract. Both companies have very high multiples due to the safety of high market share concentration and the high recurring revenue characteristic of both business models.

Opportunities

Cadence is investing a large chunk of its revenue in R&D, with the goal of improving the productivity of a limited pool of silicon design talent. With less workers available for the increasing needs of chip design, Cadence has plenty of opportunities to reduce design times, demanding higher ARPU. Since the chip design company doesn’t find more workers to design its chips, it will focus more on paying for premium features that enable its reduced team in achieving the established goals.

Cadence is infusing all its platform with AI, accomplishing productivity enhancements for its users. In the latest earnings call, the company’s management said this:

Our Cadence.ai portfolio continued gaining strong momentum with market shaping customers. Our AI powered products such as Cadence Cerebrus, SimAI and Allegro X AI are proliferating at scale. And our LLM based Design Agents powered by JedAI data platform, are showing promising results in early engagement. Cadence Cerebrus is rapidly becoming essential part of the design flow as it continued to deliver transformative PPA benefits to customers, with more than 750 tape-outs to-date and over 300 in Q4 alone.

JedAI and SimAI are interesting tools that absorb the data of the Cadence cloud design platform. With the high number of simulations processed in the platform, using SimAI, Cadence is able to infer from similar simulations the results of simulation A (A being one simulation that has related simulations in the SimAI database). JedAI executes the same process, but its focus is not on the chip simulation but on the preceding step of chip design.

Risks

Cadence is a company that has massive leadership in the analog design software. With massive investment in R&D and network effects, its leadership should prevail. Still, one of the largest and growing the fastest market is China. The latter is known for its growing capabilities in cutting-edge technology. The DeepSeek event gives strength to the previous observation. Empyrean Technology is one Chinese EDA company trying to remove the dominance of western companies in EDA and should provide serious competition for Cadence in the future.

Valuation

To understand what growth the market is pricing, I like to do a reverse discounted cash flow analysis. To model the discount rate, I use 10%. The initial revenue value is the sell-side analysts estimate for 2025. The starting free cash flow margin is 24%. Then the margin expands to 31.31% in 2034. To generate the terminal multiple, I assumed a terminal multiple of 8 that expands 1% throughout the ten years due to buybacks, resulting in a multiple of 9. Since I’m not doing the math per share and I wish to keep the model simple, I prefer to consider that the buybacks affect the total market cap (They don’t, they just affect the price per share). With the inputs, the growth is balanced to obtain the current market cap.

The model outputs an initial growth of 12.5% that reduces 5% every year until reaching 7.8% in 2034. Considering Cadence’s historical growth, the opportunities it has in reducing design times via intelligent automations, and the fact that there are just a few companies competing for market share in EDA, I conclude that the company is valued in a range of slightly undervalued to fairly valued.

Conclusion

In the long run, Cadence’s downside is limited due to the recurring and stable nature of its business and end markets, respectively. Cadence is the leader in analog semiconductor design. The sales of products belonging to this market have been increasing by low teens in the past years. Despite increasing slower than their digital counterparts, the growth in R&D that follows the increase in revenue of the analog designing companies implies more gains for CDNS. Cadence should also provide strong competition in the digital design segment. Additionally, CDNS is a strong compounder that, due to the lack of designing manpower combined with the increase in computational power and automation ability, should keep on growing in the high teens for the long future. Since I’m more aggressive on buying growth, I will rate the company as a hold, but it is a good investment for people looking for reduced downside.

data.title: Hartford MidCap Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

US equities rose for the fifth consecutive quarter to register a stellar return in 2024. Stocks rallied in November after President Donald Trump won the election and the balance of political power shifted to the Republican Party, cementing a path for Trump to enact major policy initiatives. Stocks slid in December after the Federal Reserve’s (Fed) Summary of Economic Projections revealed a markedly more gradual and shallower path of interest-rate cuts due to concerns about upside inflation risks. The Fed’s median projection signaled 50 basis points* of rate cuts in 2025.

The S&P MidCap 400 Index returned 0.34% for the period. Within the Index, five out of 11 sectors rose for the period. Energy and financials were the top-performing sectors, while real estate and materials were the bottom-performing sectors for the quarter.

Performance Summary

  • The Hartford MidCap Fund (I Share) outperformed the S&P Midcap 400 Index for the quarter.
  • Sector allocation, a result of our bottom-up stock selection process, was the primary driver of relative outperformance. Allocation effect was driven by our overweight to information technology and underweight to real estate, but partially offset by our underweight to financials and consumer staples.
  • Security selection detracted from relative performance during the quarter. Weak selection in financials and healthcare was partially offset by selection in industrials, information technology, and utilities.
  • At the issuer level, the top two relative contributors were our out-of-benchmark positions in Palantir Technologies and AppLovin, while the top two relative detractors were out-of- benchmark positions in Monolithic Power Systems and Rocket.

Positioning & Outlook

  • Through robust risk management and portfolio construction, we seek a balanced performance profile with reduced factor and style impact, which we believe can succeed relative to our peers and the Index across a variety of market environments. During the quarter, as a part of our risk management process, we initiated underweight positions in Palantir and Coinbase given both securities were outsized contributors to risk. These transactions reflect our aim to have security selection drive long-term performance, rather than any specific factor positioning. With the portfolio management transition now in place, we’re optimistic about the path forward. With the support of the entire investment platform at Wellington, we’re focused on constructing a portfolio that positions us for long-term success.
  • At the end of the period, our largest overweight was information technology, and we were most underweight to real estate and financials.

data.title: Materialise: Manufacturing Headwinds Are A Distraction
data.description:
Materialise's (NASDAQ:MTLS) share price plummeted after the company released its fourth quarter earnings, driven by disappointing results from the company's manufacturing segment. While the transition to the new ACTech facility was expected to create disruptions, an unanticipated slowdown in the European automotive market caused significant headwinds, which are expected to persist throughout 2025.

I previously suggested that Materialise was undervalued, despite the soft demand environment, with continued Medical growth and the expansion of the ACTech facility potential catalysts in 2025. While further manufacturing headwinds are obviously a negative, it doesn't materially change my opinion of the company's prospects. Materialise's Manufacturing segment has provided low growth and been marginally profitable for many years, and I don't consider it an important part of the company's value.

Market Conditions

2024 has been difficult for Materialise, due to high-interest rates, geopolitical tensions and economic weakness. Manufacturing headwinds in Europe unexpectedly picked up in the last few months of the year, causing Materialise's Q4 revenue to fall well short of guidance. The decline in activity was led by the automotive industry, with weakness expected to persist throughout 2025.

The European automotive industry has been struggling for a number of years, which suggests that current issues are more structural than cyclical. Factors that have contributed to this situation include:

  • High-energy costs
  • Stagnant labor productivity
  • Collapse of sales in China
  • Increased competition from Chinese manufacturers in Europe
  • Sales volumes that generally remain depressed relative to pre-COVID levels

Materialise Business Updates

Materialise's Medical segment was the company's primary growth engine in 2025. This was supported by a new US facility that reduced lead times, enabling Materialise to better address the trauma market. Materialise has tripled the number of trauma cases that it is treating each quarter since the plant was opened and is delivering products faster than any other provider in the market.

The cardiovascular market is another focus area, with FEops' simulation offerings strengthening Materialise's existing solution. Materialise also recently launched Mimics Flow, which is an integrated cloud solution for its Medical customers. It builds on the prior Mimics solution, with a particular focus on collaboration and automation.

Within its Manufacturing segment, Materialise continues to face headwinds in areas like prototyping and the casting of combustion engines for the automotive sector. These headwinds are expected to persist in 2025, and as a result, Materialise has taken actions to reduce its manufacturing cost base. Materialise is trying to shift from prototyping to certified manufacturing of end-use parts though. Revenue from Aerospace customers also increased 28% in 2024.

The new ACTech plant will strengthen Materialise’s position in the automotive market, particularly in EVs where there is greater demand for complex casted components for electrical drivetrains and chassis. It will also open opportunities in agriculture, mining, construction and marine vehicles where parts are typically larger and heavier. There was a planned reduction in ACTech operational capacity associated with the start-up of the new plant in the fourth quarter though.

Materialise's transition to a subscription-based model in its software segment is ongoing and this is creating revenue headwinds. Materialise's software segment has also been challenged by weak industry printer sales. Recent results from public 3D printing companies suggest that printer demand may be recovering or at least stabilizing, which should be supportive of Materialise's software revenue going forward.

Materialise is trying to expand its presence in factory management and has been adding functionality for users that scale in end-use production. Materialise also introduced Magics SDKs in the fourth quarter, enabling its customers to customize their 3D printing operations.

Financial Analysis

Materialise generated 65.7 million Euro revenue in the fourth quarter of 2024, an increase of 0.6% YoY. Manufacturing revenue declined 13% YoY in the fourth quarter, while Medical revenue was up 14% and Software revenue was fairly flat. Medtech and aerospace were bright spots in the fourth quarter, although weak prototyping demand, the new ACTech plant start-up and industrial headwinds in Europe largely offset this. The impact of macro headwinds was particularly acute given that the vast majority of Materialise's Manufacturing revenue is generated in Europe.

Materialise is currently anticipating 270-285 million Euro revenue in 2025, representing an increase of roughly 4% YoY at the midpoint. Medical and Software growth is expected in 2025, although the transition to a cloud-based subscription business model will remain a headwind. Industrial weakness in Europe is also expected to persist, which will likely ensure that Manufacturing revenue remains soft, despite an increasing contribution from ACTech.

While Materialise's software business hasn't really grown in a number of years, the transition to a subscription-based model is successfully progressing. The company had 46.9 million USD of deferred revenue related to software licenses and maintenance fees on its balance sheet at the end of 2024, an increase of 14% YoY. Recurring revenue from software maintenance and license sales, including CO-AM, increased by 19% YoY in Q4, non-recurring revenue was down 35%. Something like three quarters of Materialise's software revenue is now recurring.

Adjusted EBITDA totaled 4.3 million Euro in the fourth quarter of 2024 at a 6.6% margin, down from 8.5 million Euro in the prior year comparable quarter. The drop in profitability was attributed to the start-up of production at the new ACTech plant, industry headwinds, restructuring costs, R&D and the integration of FEops.

Around 1.5-2 million Euro of the cost increase was attributed to the integration of FEops, restructuring costs and the ACTech plant start-up, all of which should drop off going forward. R&D spend is likely to remain elevated though, with Materialise continuing to invest in its Medical business.

Cash flow from operating activities totaled 6.2 million Euro in the fourth quarter, with favorable movements in working capital offsetting cost increases. CapEx was 7.8 million Euro, which was due to the completion of the ACTech building and the installation of new machinery. While the majority of these investments are now complete, Materialise will continue to invest in additional machinery as it ramps production at the facility.

Materialise ended the fourth quarter with 102 million Euro cash on its balance sheet. The company's net cash position was 61 million Euro. This positions the company to weather any downturn, although isn't a particularly important consideration given that Materialise is likely to remain profitable going forward.

Conclusion

While Materialise's fourth quarter results were soft, the weakness was fairly isolated. Medical revenue growth should remain robust going forward, and Software growth will strengthen as the transition to a subscription model matures. Manufacturing revenue is likely to be weak in 2025, although the new ACTech facility should provide a boost as the year progresses. The Manufacturing segment remains a concern though. While it is likely necessary due to synergies with the rest of the business, Materialise's Manufacturing segment has struggled to create value on its own.

In addition to continued growth, Materialise's margins will move higher over time due to operating leverage and growth of the Medical segment. Free cash flows will also recover once the new ACTech facility is fully operational. As a result, it is not difficult to see Materialise generating something like 30 million Euro free cash flow in 2026 versus roughly a 350 million USD market capitalization. While Materialise will likely need to follow through on this potential before its valuation moves consistently higher, this is likely to happen in the next few years.

Fund performance

Institutional Class shares of Columbia Dividend Income Fund (MUTF:GSFTX) returned -1.34% for the quarter ending December 31, 2024. The fund’s benchmark, the Russell 1000 Index, returned 2.75% for the same period.

Market overview

The broad U.S. stock market finished the fourth quarter with modest gains, as gauged by the 2.75% return for the Russell 1000 Index. As the period opened, the market was anticipating that the U.S. Federal Reserve was on a course to steadily ease monetary conditions following September’s 50 basis-point decrease in the federal funds target rate range from 5.25%–5.50% to 4.75%–5.00%. However, the outcome of the November 5 presidential election raised the prospect of policies viewed as likely to increase inflation, including higher tariffs and mass deportations of undocumented individuals. The Fed went on to cut its benchmark overnight rate by 25 basis points in both November and December, leaving the target rate at 4.25%–4.50% at year end. Of greater interest to the markets, the central bank signaled the likelihood of only two additional quarter-point reductions in 2025, down from the four expected entering the fourth quarter. Against this backdrop, the bellwether 10-year Treasury yield ended December at 4.58% vs. 3.81% entering the quarter.

The November election result also removed a long-standing source of uncertainty for investors and raised hopes that government policy would shift in a more pro-growth, market-friendly direction, easing recession fears. As was the case throughout 2024, U.S. mega-cap technology companies — particularly those expected to capitalize on the increased adoption of Artificial Intelligence — remained the primary driver of both broader market returns and the relative strength for the growth style. The Russell 1000 Growth Index returned 7.07% in the quarter, outpacing the -1.98% return of the Russell 1000 Value Index. For the full calendar year 2024, the two indices returned 33.36% and 14.37%, respectively.

Within financials, outperformance was largely due to bank holdings, including JPMorgan Chase, Wells Fargo and Morgan Stanley. The steepening yield curve should help support bank net interest income (the spread between what banks pay on deposits and receive in interest on loans). In addition, the new administration is expected to take a less aggressive approach to regulating bank activity and capital requirements.

While overall positioning in consumer staples had only a marginal impact on performance, value-based retailer Walmart was among the top individual contributors for the quarter. Walmart has continued to see its market share and results benefit as a wider range of consumers focuses on value in the wake of recent inflation and as its online sales grow. In addition, Walmart’s digital advertising and marketplace offerings are growing rapidly, boosting the company’s margins. The positive contribution from Walmart was offset by exposure to Mondelez International, which was among the top detractors, as margins for the global snack company have been pressured by higher cocoa prices.

Positioning with respect to consumer discretionary weighed most heavily on relative performance, most notably a lack of exposure to non-dividend payers Tesla (TSLA) and Amazon (AMZN). Overweight positions in Home Depot and McDonalds (MCD) detracted as well. Sentiment with respect to Home Depot suffered from the rise in mortgage rates, which is a negative for housing-related stocks. However, the company maintains a dominant market-share position with promising long-term repair and remodel tailwinds. Shares of McDonalds sagged on headlines around an E. Coli outbreak linked to onions served on its hamburgers. The company reacted quickly to address the problem, and we believe this issue has been resolved.

Selection within information technology also detracted, primarily driven by a lack of exposure to AI-driven chipmaker Nvidia (NVDA) and iPhone manufacturer Apple (AAPL), both of which do not meet the fund’s dividend-focused thresholds. In addition, overweight positions in semiconductor equipment companies KLA and Lam Research (LRCX) constrained performance. Both are longstanding fund holdings that we believe are well positioned with strong long-term growth drivers and high barriers to entry. However, U.S. government restrictions on sales of advanced chipmaking equipment to China have impacted investor sentiment around semiconductor equipment stocks. Exposure to chipmaker Analog Devices (ADI) also detracted amid weak current demand in industrial and auto end markets. These detractors were partially offset by exposure to Broadcom, which has seen its results benefit from rising sales of customized semiconductor chips used in AI applications. In addition, holdings of enterprise networking company Cisco (CSCO) and hyperscaler and enterprise productivity giant Microsoft proved additive, as both companies are positioned to benefit from the exploding growth in AI.

Within communication services, a lack of exposure to Google (GOOG,GOOGL) parent Alphabet and streaming service provider Netflix (NFLX) detracted. Alphabet does not meet the fund’s dividend threshold while Netflix pays no dividend. An overweight to Comcast (CMCSA) detracted, as the broadband provider has been shedding subscribers and experiencing delays in launching a new Universal theme park. These issues are expected to improve during 2025.

Within health care, holdings of Elevance Health and UnitedHealthcare weighed on performance. Results for the managed care industry have suffered from rising medical cost trends relative to premiums received in government Medicare and Medicaid programs, which led to margin pressure and negative earnings revisions in the quarter. Reimbursements are expected to improve later in 2025 and 2026. Other detractors included pharmaceutical companies viewed as more defensive holdings against a backdrop of generally bullish sentiment, including Johnson & Johnson, AbbVie and Merck (MRK). At a more company-specific level, AbbVie experienced a failed clinical trial for a new schizophrenia treatment it had acquired the rights to for approximately $9 billion, while Merck continued to experience lower shipments of its blockbuster human papillomavirus vaccine Gardasil, driven by a decline in orders from its distribution partner in China.

Finally, within industrials, shares of defense contractors Lockheed Martin (LMT) and Northrup Grumman (NOC) dipped on uncertainty over the impact of anticipated cost-cutting initiatives that may emerge from the incoming administration’s new Department of Government Efficiency.

Outlook

We continue to consistently implement an investment process that identifies companies that can be winners over the long term, rather than chasing the current market preference.

We invest in high-quality companies with sustainable free cash flow, defensible profit margins, capital discipline and strong balance sheets, in the belief that such companies have the potential to increase dividends throughout the economic cycle and through up and down markets. This focus seems especially timely given the potential for a more gradual decline in market interest rate levels. We believe the strategy’s consistent focus on quality factors should benefit relative performance over full market cycles.

data.title: Red Cat: New Developments Could Make This Modern Warfare Stock Fly
data.description:
The ongoing war between Russia and Ukraine has demonstrated the importance of drone technology in modern warfare. Drones take on a variety of roles in the battlefield such as reconnaissance and target acquisition all the way to being used as a form of maneuverable aerial bomb when equipped with explosives. The use of a large number of relatively inexpensive drones by Ukraine has allowed its relatively smaller army to hold off and at times defeat its much larger foe.

It is in this context that I am analyzing US drone manufacturer Red Cat (NASDAQ:RCAT) as a pure play to take advantage of the trend of drone warfare. The company is still relatively small at less $1 billion market cap despite the huge stock run-up. But as I will discuss, the potential for this company is fairly large.

Red Cat Capitalizing on a Large Military Drone Market

I am sure that Ukraine’s success with drone warfare has not gone unnoticed by the US defense industry. In fact, Tesla (TSLA) CEO Elon Musk has gone so far as to call the Pentagon's prized fifth-generation stealth jet, the F-35 Lightning "obsolete" and "inefficient". I can imagine a future where countless small drones with missile payloads could overwhelm a conventional military. I believe that companies like Red Cat are moving the world to this new reality. Therefore, the total future market size for these types of drones is massive. For example, in the Ukraine-Russia conflict, the former is said to have lost (and therefore needs to create/ purchase) approximately 10,000 drones per month.

According to an article by Fortune Business Insights, the military drone market size was projected to have grown to $16.07 billion in 2024. It is forecasted to grow at a CAGR of 13.15% reaching $47.16 billion by 2032. The market share of North America is about 36% and specifically looking at the US market alone, the estimate is for a market size of $10.7 billion by 2030. It is a fairly large market for Red Cat and assuming that it gets even a small portion of this TAM could mean billions in revenue and substantial gain for shareholders.

The most recent US Army contract win by Red Cat is extremely significant for the company. Last November, Red Cat was selected as the winner of the US Army’s Short Range Reconnaissance (SRR) Program of Record after a rigorous, multi-tranche competitive process. Starting all the way back in 2022, this selection was made based on rigorous technical requirements, extensive testing, and evaluations by the Army. The field started with 37 drone manufacturers from which Red Cat was ultimately selected. Apart from the Revenue this generates, this contract win showcases the technological capabilities of Red Cat’s Black Widow drone. In addition to the (SRR) Program, Red Cat also secured a $518,000 order for its Edge 130 drone from the Army National Guard and another unnamed US Government Agency.

In my eyes, these orders sort of act as a “seal of approval” regarding the company’s technology and capabilities. With a successful US Army contract under its belt, this will open doors for the company for other US defense department contracts. International customers, particularly NATO allies like Germany and the UK which are increasing defense spending, could be another group of customers for Red Cat as well. From the management’s earnings call;

So first and foremost, since the SRR announcement about three weeks ago, we have been inundated by customers requesting quotes and pricing and response to RFPs, both through DoD, USG, and internationally. And I think as of Friday, the quotes that have gone out the door right around 14.7 million, this is no SRR customers, this is all Black Widow customers. Preponderance of those are going be U.S. DoD. They're doing budgeting now. So when the [CR ends], they can pull the trigger on that. So we're super excited about that. We're getting great demand signals globally on the new platform. So we're super excited about that.

Competitive advantage among other Drone Manufacturers

Red Cat is an integrated hardware and software company specializing in military drones. While there are many other drone manufacturers, in my view, Red Cat’s technology hones in on specific military needs providing it with a competitive advantage. Through its two subsidiaries, Teal Drones and FlightWave Aerospace, Red Cat has created a “Family of Systems” i.e. different types of drones specialized to accomplish certain tasks.

Among these are the Black Widow, a small unmanned ISR (“Intelligence, Surveillance, and Reconnaissance”) system that was purchased by the US Army as discussed above. TRICHON a fixed-wing VTOL for medium-range surveillance and delivery and FANG (currently under development) which is optimized for military operations that require precision strike capabilities. The goal of these systems is to supply low-cost, portable, field-repairable, and recoverable drones which has been deemed a key priority by the Trump administration. As mentioned by management in the company’s latest earnings report;

So there's been a lot of talk from this administration about how they don't like big exquisite machines that cost tens of millions of dollars when you can buy drones like ours that are probably more capable than any drone ever fielded and now it fits in your rucksack and cost a lot less. We fit perfectly into the efficiency models to make soldiers better, safer, and more lethal. And this administration has said many times now, specifically in the last couple of days, how they want to build the largest drone and robot army in the world

In line with maintaining and developing this technological edge, Red Cat has announced a partnership with Palantir (PLTR) to enhance its Black Widow drones with autonomous visual navigation and "warp speed" capabilities. The latter’s Visual Navigation (VNav) software can be used to upload satellite imagery for accurate navigation into the drones themselves i.e. fully offline. The technology allows drones to navigate terrain by using sophisticated computer vision techniques and matching what it “sees” against satellite imagery. Thus, this technology is critical for use in areas where GPS or radio signals is unavailable or unreliable. Red Cat will also start utilizing Palantir's manufacturing OS, “Warp Speed”, to improve the former’s production process and allow it to scale up production rapidly. This particular collaboration with a firm like Palantir strengthens Red Cat’s technological edge over its competitors.

Red Cat Faces Liquidity and Dilution Risks

In terms of key risks facing Red Cat, liquidity risks and quite simply running out of money is one of the top ones. As a firm focused on high growth with minimal revenue coming in ($1.5 million for fiscal Q2 2025 and $4.3 million year-to-date). Red Cat may run out of money before it fully develops its products and gets them out to market. Based on the latest quarter report, Red Cat has total cash in its balance sheet of $4.6 million along with existing debt obligations of $356,964 and $11.9 million in convertible notes. It closed a deal to get an additional $6 million in convertible debt giving it a total of $10.6 million in cash and $18.3 million in Total Debt (although the vast majority of this debt is convertible).

Red Cat continues to raise money via convertible debt and warrants. The company announced that it received $4.875 million from previously issued warrants. These were equivalent to about 750,000 shares of common stock with a price of $6.50. Red Cat also recently secured an additional $20 million in debt financing from The Lind Partners. This debt is convertible at $16.15 per share and comes with an unspecified number of warrants.

In addition to this, Red Cat has also applied for $58 million in debt financing from the Department of Defense Office of Strategic Capital. The cash from the company’s capital-raising activities will be used to scale up production and further develop its drone technology. Given the importance of having the best drone technology for the future of warfare, I think there’s a good chance of Red Cat obtaining this loan. The good thing about Department of Defense loans is that these tend to have lower rates and are not convertible and therefore non-dilutive.

For the last six months, the net cash used in operating activities amounted to roughly -$12.5 million. Annualizing this figure implies a Net Cash from operations “burn” of about -$25 million. The fact that the company has this level of cash burn strains Red Cat’s liquidity highlighting the need for more new funding. I don’t expect this amount of “cash burn” to decrease and may actually even increase as the company is solely focused on building the products and the business. Thus it will need to raise additional capital to fund this growth. Red Cat has been utilizing the issuance of convertible debt therefore making future dilution, should growth pan out, a high probability. In other words, if RCAT stock keeps on rising as the market prices in future growth, convertible debt holders can swap out their debt for new equity at favorable prices. This will increase the number of shares and reduce the ownership stake of current holders.

Valuation and Conclusion

Given these new developments, I think that Red Cat may be trading at a decent value. Using Seeking Alpha’s quant ratings, the company has scored both an “F” on Price / Sales TTM and forward as well as Enterprise Value to Sales ratios. However, these numbers do not yet reflect the recent developments in the company. In the latest earnings call, management has guided for revenues between $80 and $120 million in the next year. At the current share price the company is trading at $690.6 million in Enterprise Value this would give it an EV/Sales Ratio of 6.9x

Comparing Red Cat with some of its direct peers as seen on the chart below we can see that the company is trading at a bit of a premium. However, it is not wildly expensive as it is only 49% higher than the average EV/Sales ratio of 4.6x for its direct peers such as Unusual Machines (UMAC) and Ondas Holdings (ONDS) which have ratios of 7.4x and 5.3x respectively.

Given the transformational nature of the army contract and the Palantir partnerships, it is my view that there is a potential that we could see Revenues accelerate quickly in the next few years. For sure an investment in Red Cat would carry a higher than average risk due to execution risk and the aforementioned dilution risk. It is in my view though that the rewards from the growth potential outweigh these risks. I am giving the company a speculative “Buy” rating.

data.title: 3 Things - State Of The Markets
data.description:
Here are some things I think I am thinking about.

1) The Walmart Warning

Markets fell over -1.5% on Friday as global PMIs disappointed on the downside and Walmart (WMT) warned about their earnings for the year. How worrisome is this? I think Chris Williams of S&P summed it up best when he said:

“The upbeat mood seen among US businesses at the start of the year has evaporated, replaced with a darkening picture of heightened uncertainty, stalling business activity and rising prices. Optimism about the year ahead has slumped from the near-three-year highs seen at the turn of the year to one of the gloomiest since the pandemic. Companies report widespread concerns about the impact of federal government policies, ranging from spending cuts to tariffs and geopolitical developments. Sales are reportedly being hit by the uncertainty caused by the changing political landscape, and prices are rising amid tariff-related price hikes from suppliers.”

I’ve been pretty clear on this one so far. My baseline view is that Trump’s policies are more likely to be disinflationary than anything else, with the risk of actual deflation if they cut too much, too fast. In total, the combination of reducing immigration, tariffs and government job cuts all end up with the potential to reduce aggregate demand in the short run. And if they cut a lot more of the government than expected, then we could start to see a very material impact to future labor reports.

The comments from S&P are especially interesting to me because suppliers are trying to front-run the tariffs, but consumer demand is softening, which means firms are likely to have trouble passing on any price hikes. In other words, firms are trying to raise prices in anticipation of higher input prices, but there’s a good chance they aren’t going to be able to pass on those input costs if the demand isn’t there.

How worrisome is all of this for now? I don’t think we should be overreacting just yet. My recession rule isn’t showing any signs of increased recession risk just yet, but as I noted, there is a good chance this all starts to impact future labor reports, so we’ll be keeping a close eye on both weekly jobless claims reports and the monthly employment reports to assess the near-term risks to the economy.

2) Are Foreign Stocks Finally Beating Domestic Stocks?

I loved this comment from Meb Faber about international diversification. I am paraphrasing, but he says that international investing has worked for 98% of the 50 largest countries in the last 15 years. Of course, the one place where it hasn’t worked so great is... the USA. In other words, if you’d diversified out of domestic stocks, you’d have benefitted from following global market cap allocations because the USA has trounced everything. If, on the other hand, you’d been a US-based investor, the story didn’t work out as well. You still did just fine, as Vanguard Total World has generated 12.5% per year over that period, but Vanguard Total Domestic generated 16%. So, if you lived in any other country you wanted to own US stocks, but if you lived in the US you wish you’d been only allocated to the US.

I always highlight that the main reason to own foreign stocks is to diversify domestic currency risk. The US dollar has been on a 30% tear for the last 15 years, but it’s down 1.5% so far this year. And the result is that US stocks are up 2%, while foreign stocks are up 7.2%. Is this finally the year? Is this finally the start of a period of dollar decline and foreign stock outperformance? It’s only been 6 weeks, so let’s not get ahead of ourselves, but it does highlight the benefit of owning foreign stocks when there’s domestic currency risk. And given the dollar’s huge outperformance for the last 15 years, I’d argue that this is certainly a period where some foreign diversification has the potential to add beneficial diversification over the next 15+ years.

3) The Future of Rates and Inflation

One Trump policy idea that isn’t getting enough attention is his expectation of interest rates. In the last 6 weeks, he has repeatedly stated that he will “demand” lower interest rates in the coming years. And I think he’s going to get what he wants because he’s going to install someone at the Fed in 2026 who gives him what he wants.

I know a lot of people will say the President doesn’t control the Fed or that the Fed Chief doesn’t control interest rates, but they certainly can have a lot of influence. And so, imagine a scenario where interest rates are still at 4%+ in 2026 and Powell is on the verge of ending his term. Inflation is still in the 2-3% range and still looking slightly sticky, while the economy is softening further. In that scenario, I think we get a new Fed Chief who wants to aggressively give Trump a lower and more stimulative interest rate regime. So, the way I view it is that his policies probably create some downside interest rate risk because of the potentially disinflationary risks.

To summarize all of this, I’d argue that Trump’s policies are likely to be marginally disinflationary, but even if they’re not, you’re still going to end up with lower interest rates because the Fed Chief he installs will give him lower interest rates even if inflation isn’t as low as the Powell Fed would like it to be at that time.
