data.title: BJ's Restaurants: Hot Pizookies, Frozen Pipeline
data.description:
Pizookie Meal Deal Arrived to Save the Day

It appears that BJ's Restaurants (NASDAQ:BJRI) is gradually relearning how to drive traffic in a market where guests are seeking bargains. It hasn’t been a walk in the park. From Pizookie to Pizookie, BJ’s managed to report better-than-expected results for the fourth quarter.

Since its ill-fated foray into value platforms with the Pizookie Pass, the full-service restaurant chain has gone back to basics. It launched a prix fixe offering for lunch, offering affordable price points for guests looking for a convenient and tasty meal.

There are about eight options of different dishes, ranging from Italian pasta with chicken parmigiana to a mini Deep Deep pizza with house salad. But of course, the Pizookie couldn't be left out. The difference with the price platform is that this iconic dessert from BJ's Restaurants is already included in the price of $13.

But wait a minute! It's important to note that the Pizookie served in the promotion is the personal Pizookie, which is about half the size of a regular Pizookie.

If you think it’s not worth it, think again.

If we ordered the items separately and at the regular menu price, the promotional items would come out to an average of $14. Combine that with the price of the Pizookie and you’d be spending about $22 on this lunch. With the special offer, we’re getting a discount of about $9!

We had some color on how this offering performed since early September. BJ's Restaurants said they outperformed Black Box Casual Dining by approximately 500 basis points on average, even reporting same-store sales up 4% year-over-year in the first few weeks of October.

But it doesn’t stop there. In Q4 FY 2024, BJ’s Restaurants reported same-store sales up 5.5% year over year, likely picking up momentum in November, a much higher-than-average month for the industry. Although it didn’t disclose it, BJ’s saw weaker comps for traffic, which had been declining year-over-year since Q2 FY 2023 but began to decline in the mid-single digits in Q4 FY 2023.

I said that BJ’s Restaurants had likely seen weaker comps ahead and that we had seen a bottom in same-store sales. That appears to be exactly what happened.

In short, these three factors were the biggest drivers of same-store sales for Q4:

  1. Pizookie Meal Deal attracting bargain-hunting guests to full-service restaurants. Note that the promotion was ideal for dessert lovers, with significant discounts but higher prices;
  2. A particularly strong quarter and well above the restaurant industry average for the biennium comprising 2023 and 2024. You probably saw this in the Restaurant Recap; and
  3. Softer comparisons, but mainly traffic. They stopped growing year-over-year five periods ago and have been declining ever since. BJ's Restaurants tries to compensate for this drop in traffic by increasing prices, which are losing their effectiveness now. Targeting traffic seemed like the ideal strategy now.

Let's look at the graph to better understand this:

Moving on to smaller drivers, we had the Spooky Pizookie. This was a seasonal dessert from BJ's Restaurants that returned after customer feedback. We know that Halloween 2024 was a boom time for restaurants, right in the middle of October and November. Even Papa John's (PZZA) reported that October 31st itself was its highest-transaction day in the brand's history within the United States.

Speaking of beers, it's a wonderful subject, especially when you're thirsty, isn't it? Management noted that they saw tailwinds during the 25th anniversary celebration of their Belgian Grand Cru beer.

This is an award-winning label, having won the World Beer Cup in 2000 and another silver at the Great American Beer Festival in 2002. And as part of the celebration, BJ's is selling a limited-edition silver collector's cup. This ended up making a bit of a splash, especially since this is one of BJ's Restaurants' most famous handcrafted beers.

I know you liked my trivia. Here's another one! In the first six years of its existence, BJ's changed the recipe for Grand Cru for at least six years. This is in line with the Belgian tradition of changing recipes for its handcrafted beers from time to time. With customers clamoring for the award-winning version from the early 2000s, BJ's has stuck with the original recipe for at least eight years.

Pipeline Continues Slow, But Has Undergone Strategic Reviews

I'm sure many of you, like me, thought that BJ's Restaurants would open a few restaurants in FY 2024. Well, the Brookfield opening was going very well, generating reasonably good sales volume for a low DMA area. But nothing happened. That's a shame.

On the bright side, BJ's has conducted a strategic review of its restaurant portfolio and has not identified any locations that need to close. All of them are generating relatively healthy AUVs with restaurant-level margins in the mid-teens. Looking ahead, the company has decided to postpone some openings that were planned for FY 2025, reducing the potential for openings next year. They now expect to open one location year-round (Queens Creek, Arizona). The expectation is that this will pick up in FY 2026.

This is understandable. BJ's restaurant-level economics are not the best, despite short-term growth. Restaurant-level margins are 15.4%, up 100 basis points year over year. Generating AUVs of $7 million, these boxes generate a very attractive unit-level cash flow of approximately $1.08 million per year. The problem here is the same as other upscale casual dining restaurants that have high construction costs.

If you don't know, BJ's restaurants are quite expensive to build. Before the Lean Prototype, which maintains annual sales of $7 million but has a construction cost of $6 million, BJ's Restaurants was working with $7 million prototypes. That's quite a hefty sum, especially for an AUV of this size.

The implications are clear. When BJ's Restaurants opens a store, they expect to recoup their capital investment in the 5th or 6th year of operation. And I'm not even talking about the discounted payback! You could argue that Portillo's (PTLO) or Cheesecake Factory (CAKE) are more expensive per case, but look at the sales volume of those companies. Each Portillo's location in Chicagoland has AUVs of over $12 million, and the same can be said for Cheesecake Factory.

Understanding the Impact on BJ's Margins

For some time now, I have been reporting that, despite being emptier, BJ's restaurants were more economical, especially after the entry of PW Partners, an activist investor. Well, this was the strategy adopted by many other restaurants to deal with the decline in consumption. Even El Pollo Loco (LOCO) joined in. However, many of the benefits of these initiatives could not be seen while the company saw its top-line sales decline year after year. It was necessary, more than ever, to boost its sales. And that happened. Here are the improvements.

Back to basics, same-store sales across the board increased 5.5% year over year during Q4. Consider that about 100 basis points of that was driven by favorable holiday shifts. Still, 4.5% year-over-year growth isn’t something you see every day at a restaurant chain these days.

Since we had no recent restaurant openings, top-line sales grew 6.4% year-over-year to $344.3 million during Q4 FY 2024. Now, looking at the full year, since other periods BJ's Restaurants saw negative same-store sales, top-line sales grew just 1.8% year-over-year to $1.357 billion. Note that this was driven solely by same-store sales growth, as restaurant openings were not a significant driver here.

Note that now, with top-line sales growing, fixed costs have ended up being better diluted, becoming a little less relevant in relation to revenue. But consider that these fixed costs are already being reduced as the company implements its cost reduction initiatives.

Here are some of them:

  • AI-based workforce forecasting;
  • Optimization of complimentary food and beverage management;
  • Simplification of margarita registration at the POS (decreased wrong orders by 20%);
  • Improved order display in the kitchen for pickup and delivery (increased accuracy by 10%);
  • Implemented QR codes to track repairs and predict replacements;
  • Adopted preventive maintenance for kitchen equipment;
  • Deepened the shoulder-to-shoulder training in the back-of-the-house.

This not only directly reduced costs, but also helped reduce turnover among hourly employees and GMs. You may already know how reducing this metric is related to both increased traffic and same-store sales. BBI data suggests that restaurants that are fully staffed perform better. For GMs, the benefit is 2.8% year-over-year for traffic and 3.3% year-over-year for same-store sales. For hourly employees, the impact is even greater, 3.6% year-over-year for traffic and 4.6% year-over-year for same-store sales.

Virtually every cost center benefited except Food, Beverage and Packaging, which grew 40 basis points year over year and now accounts for 25.9% of total revenue. Note that this was driven by the impact of the Pizookie Meal Deal. There are eight different dishes, with costs modestly higher than the rest of the BJ's menu. We anticipated this, but it appears that the price increases have made this impact less significant.

Labor costs were 35.8%, down 70 basis points year-over-year, impacted by positive labor initiatives. They are still high, about 500 basis points above the ideal and at least 300 above the acceptable. In theory, with same-store sales growing, we will see these costs gradually being diluted, but in my opinion there is a long way to go until normality, even with wages in the industry growing at a slower pace (except for chefs).

These changes resulted in full-year EBITDA reaching $104.8 million in FY 2024, a margin of 7.72%. This is up from 6.96% in FY 2023. EBIT also grew 48.4% year-over-year to $32.5 million, with a margin of 2.39%. All of these metrics are far above what BJ's has achieved in the post-pandemic period. However, they fall short when compared to the period prior to FY 2019.

Debt and Buybacks: What Will BJ's Do With the Spare Cash?

BJ's Restaurants ended FY 2024 with long-term debt of $66.5 million and cash and equivalents of $26.1 million. Note that they deleveraged their balance sheet with $7.7 million of debt, using a higher OCF, even with a higher Capex to pay down debt. This gives us a Debt/EBITDA ratio of 2.04x. Nothing to worry about.

Also taking advantage of stronger cash flow, BJ's Restaurants repurchased 234,000 shares of common stock at a cost of $8 million. They currently have $83 million available in our share repurchase program after recently increasing the program by $50 million. They currently have 22.9 million shares outstanding.

Note that at this point, if they execute the full repurchase program, BJ's could reduce their shares outstanding by at least 2 million. However, we will see higher capex during FY 2025 due to remodeling. They plan to remodel up to 30 existing locations.

Guidance for FY 2025

  • Q1 comparable sales up close to 2%. Note that this still leaves BJ's outperforming the BBI Index for the quarter, but narrows the spread due to the weather in certain markets and the more general conservatism in consumer spending after the holidays. Management expects comps to accelerate during Q2 and Q3, but moderate during Q4 as they face this quarter. My view is that they should remain in the low single digits for Q1, slowly accelerating to mid-single digits by mid-year and perhaps a flatter Q4;
  • Adjusted EBITDA of $127 million to $137 million, representing 21.2% year-over-year growth;
  • Capital expenditures of $65 million to $75 million. In 2025, they plan to open one new restaurant and remodel up to 30 existing locations. Note that subtracting the $6 million for the new opening, that’s $1.9 million spent per restaurant on remodeling. By the end of 2025, approximately 60% of BJ’s restaurants will be recent prototypes;
  • Repurchase $40 million to $50 million of shares in 2025. If the price remains in the mid-$30s, BJ's will buy back 1.1 million to 1.4 million shares, reducing BJ's outstanding shares to 21 million.

Should I Buy This Growth Or Is It Expensive?

If we strip out non-cash items, we have EPS of $1.40. Note that this is quite different from the $0.72 EPS that GAAP tells us. Given an analyst consensus EPS of $1.67, we have a projected 19.3% year-over-year growth. That's still good, but much lower than the 131.9% year-over-year growth that the raw numbers suggest.

I say this because many may look at the PEG of 0.3 and get excited. But when we consider growth based on non-GAAP standards, we get a projected PEG that varies from 1.28 to 1.74. Therefore, I do not see EPS growth as a relevant reason to recommend buying here. On the contrary, this PEG is 20.66% higher than the industry median.

Let's make some projections based on your EPS and RPS. Remember, I'm using the Modigliani-Miller Hypothesis for Dividend Irrelevance to Firm Value to model the scenarios in the DDM:

  1. I am considering projected EPS of $1.67 for FY 2025, this will be our first cash flow to shareholders;
  2. I am projecting top-line sales of $1.4 billion for FY 2025. If I expect shares outstanding to be 21.7 million, the RPS would be $64.51;
  3. BJ's Restaurants' 5-year average P/S is 0.67. Assuming the stock trades at this level through FY 2025, we have an exit price of $43.22. This will be our second cash flow to the shareholder;
  4. The two cash flows discounted to present value under a Cost of Equity of 7.62%, we have a PT of $41.71.

That said, I expect BJ's Restaurants to continue trading in the high $30s to low $40s. Cost containment initiatives will continue to pay off, but I consider the stock still expensive for a restaurant chain that will open just one location during FY 2025 and is expected to maintain same-store sales in the low single digits year over year.

I maintain my 'Hold' rating on BJ's Restaurants with a PT of around $41. We will continue to monitor how the price behaves after the earnings release. If it rises dramatically above our PT, we may take profits. However, if it falls below the low $30s, I see room for buying. But beyond that, there are better options out there for buy-and-hold.
